summary: Stancil argues that software pricing moved from subscriptions to granular usage, and that this shift exposes a psychological gap: customers tolerate arbitrary seat fees but demand cost-justified prices for per-unit consumption. This makes dbt Labs’ business model uniquely hard—its product is popular but doesn’t “do the expensive work”—so the dbt–Fivetran deal doesn’t solve core monetization. He suggests a Cursor-like strategy: dbt could pair with a smaller, efficient database to perform some work itself and justify fair, usage-based pricing.
money_quotes: If you can sell subscriptions to your software, you won’t get asked about margins, but you only have so much flexibility about what you can charge.
And if you sell consumption, you better charge for something that sounds expensive.
A popular product, without no obvious way to sell it.
The analogy is obvious. Cursor is to Anthropic as dbt is to a database.
Put a database underneath dbt Fusion.
key_insight: Usage-based pricing forces vendors to justify margins with “expensive-sounding” work, which leaves dbt stuck unless it owns more of the computation it currently outsources to databases.
